status.body: Today, things are basically fine. I'm mostly healed up from all the tooth extractions. I've started radiation, and I'm kind of prepared for that whole process. Therapy is helping, but bringing up difficult issues kind of sucks. Eating still sucks sometimes, but I'm doing the best I can with what I have.

entries.title: Cancerous Beginnings
body: When I first discovered something was going on we were on a mini vacation and at first it seemed like it was an ingrown hair or something that would resolve itself. Years passed and it grew in size and eventually sometimes started to bleed. I would stop the bleeding, and clean it up and it would be good until next time.

During this period it was becoming increasingly annoying when people asked questions about what was on my face, so I grew a beard. That was around early 2020 so wearing a mask was partly helping to hide a growth and a growing beard. It was all a success because the questions stopped.

entries.title: A Wild Lump has appeared
body: In the early part of the month, I noticed something new. It was a lump on my neck near where the thing on my face was. At first the new thing was just a tiny lump, so I checked it each day for any changes. One thing that I discovered was if I pushed on it I would get a bit light headed. So obviously it was a problem and after a week of it growing in size and becoming more firm, I alerted my wife. We made an appointment with our primary who examined the old thing, the new thing, and other things deemed as problem areas and sent off referrals for us to see specialists.

entries.title: Testing & Diagnosis
body: We were sent to a dermatologist who immediately identified it as skin cancer, but wasn’t sure if it was a Basal Cell Carcinoma or a Squamous Cell Carcinoma. Regardless, he was doing a biopsy of the thing on my cheek that day. With a scalpel he took a chunk of it out and sent it off to a lab.

We were also sent to an ENT, and that following monday we met him where he also did a biopsy, but it was on the new lump on my neck. In that office as the second biopsy was being done a flood of emotions washed over me as I realized how much I'd fucked up.

Following the Biopsies, the whole area between the old thing and the new thing was incredibly pissed off. The old thing had never presented with pain, it would just bleed sometimes, and the new thing didn’t hurt me until I pushed on it too hard. But now they were both angry and causing pain.

My ENT sent me for a CT Scan of the neck and face. I also was scheduled for what was called a Mohs procedure on a Friday, which as it was described by the dermatologist was a procedure where they shaved off layers of tissue and checked them to see if they needed to take more skin tissue. The night before I shaved off my beard, the beard I'd had for years as my social protection. It felt like I lost a part of my identity because it was a magnificent beard. We never had that procedure done because when they saw how big the lesion on my face was and learned that there was a lump they cancelled the procedure.

We saw my ENT the following Tuesday and he was not surprised they refused to do the procedure. We still didn’t know exactly what it was because what I was being told is that a Basal Cell Carcinoma doesn’t really spread like that. My ENT was really aggressive with figuring out what it was and scheduled me for a PET scan, a CT with and without contrast, an MRI with and without contrast, and more labwork. Before I could get them though, I had to endure having all those tests declined by my medical insurance. And I swear I heard the lady on the phone chuckle as she was telling me they were declined. Apparently, this is a normal thing because I alerted my ENTs office and they submitted some additional paperwork or my ENT talked to them on the phone or something else because the tests were then approved.
The week of the tests was rough because getting all those tests done in such a short span is taxing physically, mentally, and emotionally.

entries.title: Mass Removal & New Bad News
body: We saw my ENT that following Monday for results and he still wasn’t really calling the new lump by a specific name and was being intentionally vague. It didn’t really matter if it had a name because it was getting evicted anyway. However, it needed a name, so I figured that since nearly every Steve I've ever encountered has been a giant douche canoe, I would just name it Steve.

The first half of November was rather uneventful with most of it being back and forth between the ENT and the plastic surgeon to hammer out the date for surgery. We discussed with my ENT that I was concerned about my tinnitus getting worse so he made a note to address that after surgery. We registered for surgery on my birthday and the surgery was shortly after. The surgery was scheduled to be my ENTs last case for the day so he could spend time getting everything out without feeling rushed.

Surgery started around 1p and it was odd because usually the anesthesiologist tells you to count backwards from or towards 10 and only lets you get a couple numbers, but this time I made it all the way to 10. The next thing I remember is waking up in my hospital room and asking for my wife. Some time had passed after I woke up and they finally had contacted her to let her know where I was. She saw me and ran towards the bed and got in it with me and was nearly in hysterics. I had no idea how long the surgery was or even what time it was, but having her was the best part. Well, until we set off the bed alarm because I'd gained weight all of a sudden from her sitting in the bed with me. The nurses all rushed in confused and saw what was happening and turned the bed alarm off. I was still in excruciating pain from surgery and was told they didn’t have any orders for medicine for me.

I’m going to back up a bit, because from my wife’s perspective it’s a bit different. They took me for surgery and had her hang out in the waiting area and after almost 5 hours my ENT went to talk to her to let her know that his part was finished and that the plastic surgeon was closing me up. Once the plastic surgeon was done he found her as well and brought a picture to show her how extensive it was. Nobody really called her to say I was out of surgery or let her know that I was in a room for recovery either. The security guard asked her if she was waiting for someone because she was the last person left in the waiting area. So for my wife it was quite scary because it was taking a lot longer than she thought, but neither of us really knew how long it was supposed to take.

That catches us up from my account and her account, so after she got off my bed and was making sure I was good. She called my ENT to let him know that the nurses said there were no orders for me and that I was starting to hurt a lot. He called the nurses desk and chewed them out because he’d put the orders in before surgery because he knew what I was going to go through. They found the orders and then started bringing in meds for me. The morphine helped at first but wore off within the hour. I mentioned this to my wife and she told the nurse and I got more morphine, which again wore off after an hour.

I had my wife take a picture of my face and once I wasn’t as loopy I was able to count that there were 50-60 stitches from what I could see. She then told me that she had already spoken to my ENT and he explained to her that he wasn't sure if he got it all. He said that the margins were low enough that he could stop taking facial tissue, but still high enough that he couldn't confidently say he got it all. After that she then told me that I had to have all my teeth removed because of radiation.
I remember feeling extremely angry and scared for how I would be able to feed myself. I calmed down and we started planning for that future.

In the hospital, and for a few days after we got home I ate soft foods because of the placement of the stitches. I actually enjoy hospital food, so each time they brought my tray I was excited about what I was going to get. It was all meals that had rice or potatoes, the meat was cut up really small, and in some cases the food was pureed in a blender. I remember looking at the receipt they gave me to show what I got and seeing waffles with syrup, but nowhere on the tray were there any waffles or syrup. I did have a bowl of some gelatinous brown-ish substance, so i figured i’m in a hospital, if it ends up killing me i won’t have far to go. It turns out that pureed waffles and syrup are really good, and this happens to be one of my wife’s favorite stories of me in the hospital, lol.

I'm not posting a picture, but the top incision runs from a quarter of an inch near the corner of my mouth to about an inch to the bottom of my ear. The bottom incision starts from the middle of my ear in the crease next to my sideburns and takes a path down that crease and under my ear to the hairline behind my ear and then down neck to a nearly horizontal path to my Adams apple.

I also had a drain tube coming out of my neck near the collar bone. That tube went up my neck and felt like it was behind my ear. On the end of the tube was a flexible bulb that had to be emptied periodically. It was a giant pain in the neck.

We spoke to both the ENT and the plastic surgeon multiple times over the two days we were there. Through those conversations we learned more about the masses that were removed. They were both Basal Cell Carcinomas. The BCC on my cheek was huge. It was about an inch and a half around. The BCC on my neck was a bit smaller at around an inch around, but it was also wrapped around the parotid. The ENT took the BCC off and performed a parotidectomy. He explained that he took most of it and left the anterior portion of it, and that he also took like 9 lymph nodes out as well.

I asked the plastic surgeon how many stitches he used and told him how I'd already counted and wanted to know how close I was to the total. He said he lost count and that there were hundreds of stitches in multiple layers under the skin.

The nurses for my stay had to put new bactrim every 4-6 hours. They never wiped the old bactrim off and just applied the new on top. I stared at that picture of my face for days as I started talking to friends and family and showing everyone my new face.

Once we got home, we kept applying the bactrim. My wife helped me bathe and that was really nice because I hadn't been able to get very clean because I couldn't really get much around my neck wet. That drain tube was the most difficult thing to deal with. One time, I dropped it and it tugged on the stitch that was holding it in place, and I saw stars. I then had the idea that I would use a lanyard with a badge clip to hold it up so it wouldn't get dropped. That was a game changer because now I could carry two things instead of the bulb and one thing.

A week after surgery we saw my ENT and plastic surgeon. My ENT said I was looking good and that he was happy about the progress. That same trip back to the hospital we saw the plastic surgeon and he pulled half the stitches out. The drain tube was also removed, and just so happened to be the absolute strangest feeling I've ever had.

Thanksgiving was different because I still couldn't open my mouth very wide and long trips were tricky, but I was prepared to put my whole plate in the blender and puree it if I had to. Thankfully, I didn't have to and eating like that was a nice break ahead of the turmoil that was up next.

entries.title: Radiation Intro & First Tooth Extraction
body: The first week of December saw the remaining external stitches removed. I also met with my radiation oncologist for the first time. He mentioned that we shouldn't have to remove all of my teeth, and just the problem teeth and any healthy teeth that could become damaged because of radiation in the area. If those healthy teeth were allowed to remain there was a chance they could become infected and cause a problem since radiation would render treatment ineffective. So now the plan was to remove the top front 6 because they were damaged already, and the four molars on the right side because they are in the radiation area.

Once we met with the dentist, we learned that they wanted to do the extractions in 4 sessions while I was awake. I started therapy as well to help manage everything that I still had to go through as well as what I'd already been through. What I knew is that getting the cancer masses removed was the easy part, and what I didn't know was just how hard the rest was going to be.
I also met with speech therapy for exercises to help with muscle fatigue on my face. I was basically doing most of them already by instinct, but some sounds were still tricky for me.

In the first tooth extraction session it took them an hour to remove one tooth. The dentist working on me was a resident dentist and we had to get his attending dentist to help with the removal because as they kept saying, I had strong bones. I made the joke that it was difficult because I'd been growing them for 4+ decades. The second with that day came out a bit easier, but it was still a chore and took over half an hour. When it was all done for the day they removed the two molars on the top right side and stitched everything up and packed gauze for the trip home.
Recovery from that first extraction went well and I had a few weeks break while we made it through the holiday season of Christmas and New Year's.

entries.title: Remaining Tooth Extractions
body: I think we had the most appointments in January, because my wife also had appointments for her medical stuff as well.

The next dental extraction happened early in the month. This time it was another resident dentist who started and realized that he needed the attending dentist to assist as well. The plan for the day was the last 8 teeth. We got started and once I had enough novocaine they started cutting relief flaps to expose the jaw bone underneath to make getting the teeth out easier. They kept asking if I was ok and if we should continue, and I wanted it to be over with so I just kept saying I was good to go. They kept telling me that it was just pressure and not pain, but it wasn’t their mouth they were taking healthy teeth out of. So between the drill and all the other tools they had to use my lips were chapped and my gums were sore. The top front 6 teeth were mostly simple to get out, I guess. When they started working on the bottom right molars the novocaine started wearing off. They were able to pull the first bottom right molar out completely, but they were only able to pop the crown off of the second bottom right molar. This was about the time they told me I'd reached the threshold for novocaine, and we’d have to finish up the following monday. They stitched me up and packed me full of gauze. I had more gauze on the side which was fine, but I also had gauze in the front. It was impossible to close my mouth and I couldn't really swallow very easily.

Saliva and blood kept pooling up and I needed to swallow or spit, but it was nearly impossible, lol. My wife gets me home and sits me down on the couch and rushes off to fill meds for me. As I'm sitting there watching tv I start to choke on the aforementioned saliva and blood. I rush to the bathroom and take out all the gauze and call my wife. She answers and says don’t talk I’m just down the street I'll be home in a minute, but I didn’t have the gauze in so I told her what happened and once she got home we put the gauze back in on the side but left the front out since it wasn’t bleeding any longer. The following Monday we returned and they took out the roots of the last bottom right molar and sent us home. The fucked up part is they prescribed me 3 Xanax to take before they took those remaining roots out. One the night before, one the morning of, and one after we got there. You know, because it was really rough when they pulled the 7 and a half teeth, but why wasn’t that option before they decided they were going to take 8 teeth in one sitting?

During recovery from those teeth, a hole opened up at the site where they took the top right molars. I was able to move air between my sinus cavity and mouth. It would come and go, but it never really went away. Sometimes it would be both ways, others it would act like a one way valve. This hole made it really difficult to drink, and coupled with the sharp bone fragments on the bottom right where they had to drill, it’s easy to consider my mouth a disaster. My main concern was that it would be a thing I'd have to deal with all through radiation. Poke a giant hole in a straw if you want a feeling of what it’s like to drink with a hole between your mouth and sinuses.

I had a successful hearing test which was good news because my tinnitus had been noticeably louder since the surgery. We also met with the radiation oncologist again, but this time it was to finally get started. In order to keep my head still for the 15 minutes that radiation takes they needed to make a mask. This process involved me having to make a bite block with a tongue depressor to keep my jaw in a specific spot, but also move my tongue out of the way to prevent radiating it too much. The mask was made by soaking this head and shoulder shaped frame in warm water, and then having that whole contraption in my mouth and the lady pushing this head and shoulder shaped frame on my face until she was able to clip it into the table I had to lay on. Once it was dry enough they had to push me in and out of the CT scanner to make sure everything was lined up. Then we selected a time for radiation and it would start very soon.

We told my ENT about the hole who said he could put us in the OR and do a buccal flap to fix it, but he wanted to give the dentists a chance to correct it. I was put on a liquid diet to give the buccal flap a chance to heal. The first day was fine because I was exhausted anyway, but the next day I thought I was going to starve to death. Between being on antibiotics and a liquid diet there wasn’t much that was staving off the hunger pangs. What was really killing me was that my wife had made a huge pot of Red Beans and Rice the day before the buccal flap, and I never got to eat it. So, being as hungry as I was, I went and made a big bowl of Red Beans and Rice and remembering that I'd already had pureed waffles with syrup I pulled out the blender. I then, to my wife’s horror, dumped the entire bowl of Red Beans and Rice in the blender and added water until it was a nice smooth consistency. I nuked it in the microwave, and sat down with the bowl, and after I'd eaten all of it I finally felt full after days of starving. I’d also found another lump. A lump on my left nipple to be exact, and as strange as it seems it’s a perfectly common thing for men to get. I knew this, but everyone reassured me that it was normal and off we went to the imaging center again. I think by this point I've had all the types of imaging that I want to deal with because now I have to have a mammogram. What I didn't know was that they were also going to do an ultrasound, which was fine but just a bit unexpected. Once all the tests were done they had me wait in the room for the doctor to review the results and let me know that it was just fatty tissue.
We saw the dentist and let them know what was going on and they deferred the buccal flap procedure back to my ENT so he booked an OR and the next day he did the buccal flap.

entries.title: The little hole that could
body: Over the course of the month the hole was closed, all the way until it wasn’t. The buccal flap had failed and my ENT felt terrible, but I didn't want him to feel bad because I was just on the train and checking out the sites as we passed stops along the way until the end of radiation. I was kept on antibiotics for a long time in the early part of the month to give the little hole time to close, but alas it did not. It simply wanted to be a hole, until one day when it seemed closed. Then another day it seemed closed. Then the third day it seemed closed. Despite feeling like the hole was closed it was not, plus there is a crevice where the hole is. Finally, we admitted defeat and this little hole will just close on its own, but to give the hole a fighting chance with radiation starting very soon we settled on having a plate created that would sit over the hole similar to how dentures would fit.
We also had to go back to the radiation office to make sure the mask still fit and get a new time slot for daily radiation.

entries.title: Radiation Begins
body: We visited a new dentist, specifically my ENTs personal dentist. The whole ride there I was on edge because I remembered all the torture I went through at the last dentist and it was just too much to deal with. Songs came on the radio that I would normally recite from start to finish, but I just couldn’t break out of that feeling of fear. Once we got to the dentist we waited our turn and they brought us in the back and took a little back story of what’s gone on with the teeth and the reason they had to be pulled, etc. They took some x-rays and we found out that there is a piece of a tooth still left on the upper right where the hole won’t close. I’m not sure if I was clear so I'll restate it. THERE IS A SMALL PIECE OF A ROOT OF A TOOTH LEFT WHERE THE HOLE WON’T CLOSE. Apologies, it’s just very frustrating. Anyway, the dentist comes in, we discuss what my ENT wants done and he calls him just to clarify. The dentist is examining the rest of my mouth and his nurse is mixing up the goop that’s going into the mold. It’s white, it’s viscous, and it looks like it’s going to taste like chalk. It has to be done, but mentally I'm thinking it’s going to be gross, I don't want to do it. As he scrapes the goop into the plate that will be used to make an impression I just closed my eyes and let it happen. I open my mouth and as he’s moving it towards me I catch a minty scent. He helps guide the plate in and as the goop touches my gums it feels cool, it tastes minty, it’s pleasant and not like chalk at all, lol. It takes a bit for the cast to set, but it’s out shortly after it’s in. We're told we can pick up the bridge plate in the coming days when we come to radiation.
